Instead of the `get_crypto_algorithms()` approach, I think it might be simpler to just use a set to define the cryptography-supplied algorithms.

Something like:

```
requires_cryptography = set([
    'RS256', 'RS384', 'RS512', 'ES256', 'ES384', 'ES521',
    'PS256', 'PS384', 'PS512'
])
```

Then instead of `if not has_crypto and algorithm in get_crypto_algorithms()`, you could simply check `if algorithm in requires_cryptography`.

In such case, we are never actually sure what the intended value type is -- a term value can be any of int, unicode, or bytes, depending on context (excluding more exotic term.value types not relevant here).  It is safe to do simply keep the (presumed bytes) token as a value.

However, we could normalize a token into a title in this generated (and temporary) term.  We cannot assume that a token is anything more than 7-bit ASCII (per description in zope.schema.interfaces.ITokenizedTerm).  We can also try to normalize sane unicode from ASCII by `urllib.unquote(token).decode('utf8')` (Python 3 equivalent is `urllib.parse(token)`).

My recommendation is that I leave the value as a token, but try to normalize a title:

```python
_token_parse_py3 = getattr(urllib, 'parse', None)
_token_parse_py27 = lambda token: urllib.unquote(token).decode('utf8')
_token_parse = _token_parse_py3 or _token_parse_py2
parse = lambda token: _token_parse(token)

#...
SimpleTerm(token, title=parse(token))
```

This is really interesting. The subscribe() and unsubscribe() methods return immediately, so afterwards 1) the subscription won't be finished yet and 2) you don't know if an exception will occur.
The twisted way is to return a Deferred and do 1) addCallback for when the subscription has finished and 2) addErrback for when it has thrown an exception.

Now the question is, what should service.subscribe do?

- Just return the Subscription (current behavior): User can't react to events. :x:
- Just return the Deferred: User doesn't get the Subscription. :x:
- Return a (Subscription, Deferred) tuple: Changes the method signature. :x:
- Return a Subscription with the Deferred as an attribute: Possible, but unintuitive (and probably cumbersome to implement) :white_check_mark:
- Return a Deferred with the Subscription as an attribute: Possible, but unintuitive :white_check_mark: 
- Return a Deferred which passes the Subscription to the callback: Intuitive, but user gets the Subscription only after subscribe call has returned :white_check_mark:
- Return a Deferred which passes the Subscription to the callback, and has the Subscription as an attribute: Like the above, but the user can get the Subscription directly if he wants to :heavy_check_mark:

So my favored option is the last one.

Nick, great questions.

I thought a bit more about this...

The metadata we need here isn't in the package.json.

* The primary metadata we is the *location* in the Python package of the
JavaScript code. People could put npm packages anywhere in their python
package.
* Because of the typical way that people are using npm with Python
packages, they are often putting the pakage.json at the top-level of the
repo and then using webpack/browserify to copy the final build into the
Python package. Because of this, the package.json often isn't even in the
installed python package.

An example that shows both of these things is here:

https://github.com/jdfreder/jupyter-lightsaber

Summary: while I am 100% on board with us moving to npm, I don't think we
should go that way for the 4.2 nbextensions.

Cheers,

Brian

> I chimed in on this in the meeting, but perhaps starting to adopt the npm
> opinion now would be in order. Let's please not keep making new canonical
> locations and structures for the same string.
>
> Adopting this approach, once little extensions grow up and leave the nest,
> they already have all of their metadata ready to go, and could in turn be
> used by other kernels.
>
> Instead of _jupyter_nbextension_paths, perhaps
> _jupyter_nbextension_packages, where each of the paths returned must
> contain a package.json. In each of these, go ahead and claim the jupyter
> namespace, and have the dictionary that is currently returned by the python
> magic name instead be stored here.
>
> Here is an extreme way to encode all that information:
>
> # __init__.pydef _jupyter_nbextension_paths():
>     # yeah, cuz windows
>     return [
>         ["static", "some-extension"]
>     ]
>
> # package.json
> {
>   "name": "some-extension", # this is the install destination name
>   "version": "0.1.0",
>   "jupyter": {
>     "4": { # just be ready
>       "nbextension": {
>         "section": "notebook",
>         "src": ["."], # path bits, relative to this file
>         "require": "dist/index",
>       }
>     }
>   }
>   # ... all the other package stuff
> }
>
> Granted, this would be a bit weird for those of us that:
>
>    - have "mixed" repos that have package.json and setup.py at the same
>    level
>       - *stripping out package.json is a pretty gnarly anti-pattern as we
>       are starting to think about versioning*
>    -
>
>    use one package.json to configure multiple extensions
>    - *this is not the node way...*
>       - *but there's nothing stopping you from keeping this pattern, and
>       just having/building extra package.jsons*
>
>    Starting to reduce the amount of redundancy and freedom of choice (to
>    fail) in configuration would probably be worth it to me.
>
> —

NCHW format on a model level is not easily enforceable and thus brings about nightmares in terms of compliance checking. ONNX, as it stands today, only enforces node-level input format, which does not translate to any restrictions on model level format. C.f. my comment copied from the thread discussing Dimension Denotation:


1. > ONNX as a standard will keep as it is to support only NCHW.

    We need to be careful about what you mean. There are no operations supporting NHWC format in ONNX; however, that does not preclude models from supporting NHWC format input. In fact, you cannot preclude such use case as long as you have a **transpose** in the operator set because you can always transpose the input once and enter the NCHW world thereafter. Thus, unless you explicitly pattern match and prohibit these cases in the checker, there is no way to prevent a user from creating  ONNX **models** that accept NHWC input but uses NCHW **operators**. Thus it is my opinion that 
    > This means all ONNX models should be in NCHW format,

    does not hold true as an implication from your previous statement.

No, after this change Optimize doesn't have ownership on the ModelProto.

But as I have explained in the discussion in "optimize.h", in case the caller gets ModelProto from somewhere else, using the unique_ptr in the optimizer interface would force the caller to make another copy (and causes more memory usage).
